coredata.dc:title: Waste biomass-derived CQDs and Ag-CQDs as a sensing platform for Hg2+ ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Rajdeep
Singh, Jagpreet
Kathuria, Deepika
Matharu, Avtar Singh
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a facile method for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticle carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs) nanocomposites from syzygium cumini leaves by pyrolysis treatment for the sensing of Hg2+ ions is reported. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy (217 nm), FTIR, XRD and (HR) TEM. The CQDs measured 4 ± 1 nm (diameter) with 0.32 nm interplanar spacing. The CQDs solution showed bright green photoluminescence upon exposure to UV-light. AgNPs-CQDs exhibited Hg2+ selective colorimetric sensing properties (423 nm) over a wide pH range (4-10). Thus, use of CQDs, especially those generated from waste biomass with in-situ reducing and stabilizing agent, avoids the use expensive and toxic cadium sulfide, cadium selenide and zinc sulfide. The work encapsulates the use of a waste as renewable feedstock (green chemistry principles 1 and 7), avoids the use of toxic reagents and auxiliaries (green chemistry principles 3 and 8), and links to SDG 6 – water and sanitation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Carbon quantum dots
Syzygium cumini
Ag NPs
Colorimetric sensing
Mercury

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of silver nanoparticle modified carbon quantum dots (Ag-CQDs)
Procedure for spectrophotometric determination of Hg2+
Metal ion sensing potential of Ag-CQDs NCs
Impact of the different reaction parameters
Effect of concentrations of Hg2+ ions

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic shows the step-wise procedure for the preparation CQDs.
Spectroscopic analysis of prepared CQDs: (a) UV–vis spectra shows the absorption band with corresponding electronic transitions, and (b) FTIR results deciphers the surface chemistry.

Morphological analysis of prepared CQDs: (a) XRD pattern with crystal planes, (b–c) HR-TEM images of at different scale bar (inset shows particle size histogram and interplanar d-spacing respetively), and (d) SAED pattern.
Spectroscopic analysis of prepared Ag-CQDs NCs: (A) UV–vis spectra of AgNPs with respect to CQD AgNPs and (B) FTIR spectrum of CQD AgNPs.
Sensing characteristics of prepared Ag-CQDs NCs: (a) Instant and (b) after 24 h UV–vis spectra of Ag-CQDs NCs with different metal ions.
Interference study with other metal ions to check the selectivity.
Sensing performance of prepared Ag-CQDs NCs towards mercury at different pH.
Sensing performance of prepared Ag-CQDs NCs with different concentration of mercury.

head.ce:title: Waste biomass-derived CQDs and Ag-CQDs as a sensing platform for Hg2+ ions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University Centre For Research and Development

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a facile method for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticle carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs) nanocomposites from syzygium cumini leaves by pyrolysis treatment for the sensing of Hg2+ ions is reported. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using UV–visible spectroscopy (217 nm), FTIR, XRD and (HR) TEM. The CQDs measured 4 ± 1 nm (diameter) with 0.32 nm interplanar spacing. The CQDs solution showed bright green photoluminescence upon exposure to UV-light. AgNPs-CQDs exhibited Hg2+ selective colorimetric sensing properties (423 nm) over a wide pH range (4-10). Thus, use of CQDs, especially those generated from waste biomass with in-situ reducing and stabilizing agent, avoids the use expensive and toxic cadium sulfide, cadium selenide and zinc sulfide. The work encapsulates the use of a waste as renewable feedstock (green chemistry principles 1 and 7), avoids the use of toxic reagents and auxiliaries (green chemistry principles 3 and 8), and links to SDG 6 – water and sanitation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic heavy metal ion pollution is a major worldwide environmental concern with negative health consequences (Janani and Gurunathan, 2022); (Rajendran et al., 2022). Mercury is one of the most harmful elements in the environment owing to tremendous affinity for thiol groups in proteins and enzymes in the human body, causing problems with the brain, kidneys, and central nervous system(Hu and Zhao, 2020; Alengebawy et al., 2021; Janani and Gurunathan, 2022; Zaynab et al., 2022). Thus, there is a growing demand for easy/simple, quick, and cost-effective heavy metal detection and monitoring methods in the environment(Climent et al., 2009).

Heavy metal ions may now be detected, sensed, and imaged due to advances in nanotechnology (Singh et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). Colorimetric sensors, in particular, have a distinct advantage due to their ease of use, speed, and high sensitivity, allowing for live, real-time qualitative or semi-quantitative research (Praveen Kumar et al., 2016). For the specific measurement of Al3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ ions in polluted water, multiple colorimetric sensors based on Ag NPs and Au NPs coated with compounds such as sulfosalicylic acid, glutathione, vitamin B, L-cysteine, N-acetyl-L-cysteine, L-cysteine, thiomalic acid have been developed. (Tian et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Oyarzún et al., 2021; Park et al., 2021; Pinheiro et al., 2021). Notably, green synthesized Ag NPs can detect potentially harmful metal ions over a wide pH range of 2.0–11.

Due to their unique features, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with sizes below 10 nm have received a lot of attention in recent years (Ansari and Masoum 2021; Cai et al., 2021; Zhao and Tan 2021; He et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022). Their most essential and notable characteristic is their relatively intense photoluminescence, which is dependent on size, excitation wavelength, and surface functionalization. CDQs outperform semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs) in terms of thermal stability, chemical inertness, water solubility, and biocompatibility. As a result, CQDs are excellent sensing and bioimaging alternatives to semiconductor QDs and organic fluorescent dyes. To date, luminescence-based detection in conjunction with CQDs has been the most widely used technology for quantifying different metal ions such as Hg2+ (Li and Li 2021; Raj et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2022; Vijeata et al., 2022).

In general, top-down or bottom-up methods such as electro-oxidation, acid-assisted chemical oxidation, arc discharge, electrochemical technique laser ablation, hydrothermal, solvothermal, and pyrolysis methods are used to make CQDs(He et al., 2022; Raikwar 2022; Sun et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). However, these methods either necessitate complex, expensive equipment or are chemically intensive. Therefore, green/sustainable process should be adopted for the preparation of CQDs (Singh et al., 2020).

Herein, we report the use of pyrolyzed leaves of syzygium cumini, commonly known as Malabar plum, Java plum, black plum, jamun or jambolan, as a green carbon source in the synthesis of silver nanoparticle modified carbon dots (AgNPs-CQDs) for the detection of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 1 ). As highlighted earlier, the synthesis of CQDs is well-known in the literature but less so with pyrolzed leaves of syzygium cumini for mercury sensing. Thus, use of CDQs, especially those generated from waste biomass with in-situ reducing and stabilizing agents, avoids the use expensive and toxic cadium sulfide, cadium selenide and zinc sulfide. Furthermore, the use of CDQs negates the use of fluorescent dyes, as an auxiliary, which are often highly conjugated structures with inherent toxicity. The work encapsulates the use of a waste as renewable feedstock (green chemistry principles 1 and 7), and avoids the use of toxic reagents and auxiliaries (green chemistry principles 3 and 8). Global relevance can be further eluded to SDG 6 and a need for cleaner, safer drinking water. Thus, rapid and facile detection of pollutants becomes paramount.

Fresh syzygium cumini plant leaves (5 g) were washed with deionzed (DI) water (100 mL), cut (scissors) into small pieces, transferred in to a eaves crucible and pyrolyzed (muffle furnace, 300 °C, 3 h). Following cooling to room temperature, the pyrolyzed material was crushed into a powder form with a pestle and mortar and placed in a container until needed. AgNO3, KCl, Cu(NO3)2.3H2O, HgCl2, MnCl2.4H2O, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, NaOH Zn(CH3COO)2, AlCl3, Co(NO3)2.6H2O, NaCl, Cd(NO3)2.4H2O, K2CrO4, FeCl3.3H2O, and HCl were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (now Merck Ltd). H2O2 was obtained from Fisher Scientific Ltd. All glassware was thoroughly cleaned and washed with freshly made aqua regia (3:1, HCl: HNO3) and rinsed with deionized water.

UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. A Bruker Alpha-T FT-IR spectrometer was used to obtain FT-IR spectra. TEManalysis was executed on a Hitachi (H-7500) instrument operated at 200 kV. A Metrohm 827 pH meter was used to take all of the pH readings.

A mixture of pyrolyzed, ground, syzygium cumini leaves (Syzygium cumini L., often known as Indian blackberry or Jamun, is a polyembryonic variety of the Myrtaceae family. Syzygium cumini is an evergreen tropical tree endemic to the Indian subcontinent. We used the powdered leaves of Syzygium cumini (since they are prevalent in India) (2 g), DI water (100 mL) and H2O2 (2.5 mL, 30%) was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The resultant yellow-coloured slurry was centrifuged (10,000 rpm) for 15 min. After centrifugation, the pellet was discarded whilst the supernatant was re-centrifuged. The pellet was once again discarded whilst the supernatant was isolated, dried (oven, 100 °C, 24 h), to afford the desired CQDs, as a dark brown powder (500 mg, 25 wt%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silver nanoparticle modified carbon quantum dots (Ag-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Ag-CQDs were prepared using 1:5 ratio of AgNO3 and CQDs in 2 m L of DI water. AgNO3 (5 mL, 10−2 M) was added, dropwise, to a stirred mixture of CQDs (8 mg) in DI water (5 mL). The Ag NP CQDs composites were made from two different solutions i.e. AgNO3 in water (5 mL, 10−2 M) and CQDs in water (8 mg, 5 mL) and thus for the final preparation of Ag NP CQD composites, the concentration of AgNO3 was 8.45 mg/mL and CQDs was 1.6 mg/mL. The colour of the solution altered from colourless to reddish brown, indicating that AgNO3 had been completely reduced and Ag-CQDs NCs were formed.

The presence of functional groups such as –OH, –NH2 on the surface of carbon dots act as reducing agent for the reduction of Ag + to Ag0 (Jin et al., 2016). The –NH2 groups were predicted to connect to the Ag NPs' exteriors, leading the –OH and –COO– groups to induce the stability of Ag-CQDs NCs in an aqueous phase by electrostatic repulsive interactions. Nevertheless, carbon dots act as capping agents, keeping Ag NPs stable in the aqueous media for at least 45 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for spectrophotometric determination of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: An aliquot (20 μL) of Hg2+ (1–1000 ppm) was mixed with Ag-CQD solutions (2 mL) with gentle shaking. The UV–visible spectra of prepared solution were recorded after reaction for 2 min. A colour change from brownish yellow to colourless was indicative of sensing.

Fig. 2 a shows the UV–Visible spectra of produced CQDs, which shows an absorption edge at 217 nm, which indicates π-π*transition of the coupled CC units correlating to the carbon-core.(Zhang et al., 2014; Jlassi et al., 2020).

To investigate the structures and functional groups of the as-prepared CQDs, Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) was performed as shown in Fig. 3 b. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs have been recorded using Bruker Alpha-T FT-IR spectrometer. The CQDs show an intense absorbance band with a maxima at 3565 cm−1, which corresponds to the O–H vibrational stretch typical of alcohols, phenols and carboxylic acid. A strong, possible double absorbance band at approximately at 3100 cm−1 may be due to the N–H stretching mode corresponding to either a primary amine or amide. The typical C–H stretching vibration is observed at 2869 cm−1. A strong CO stretching vibration is seen at 1714 cm−1 which may correspond to a carbonyl of an amide, ester, carboxylic acid or ketone. The CQDs have a strong absorbance band at 1324 cm−1, which may be an N–H bending vibration (Park et al., 2016).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted to investigate the crystalline nature of green synthesized CQDs as described in Fig. 3a. Debye Scherer's equation was used to calculate the mean crystallite size of biogenic produced CQDs.
d =
0.89 λ
β cos θ
whereby d is the estimated scale diameter of NPs, 0.89 is a constant crystallographic form factor, λ is the X-ray light input wavelength, θ is the Bragg's diffraction angle, and β is the angular effective area at half maximum (FWHM) of Peak position seen at diffraction angle 2θ.
XRD pattern depicted a broad amorphous hump at 2θ = 21.6° along with a weak peak at 2θ = 35.7° corresponding to (002) and (101) planes of amorphous carbon (Dong et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012).

The study of morphology, interplanar space, size and nano crystalline structure of prepared CQDs is estimated by HR-TEM. The transmission electron microscope is very effective device in material science. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of thebiogenic CQDs, shows that these carbon dots are separated from each other and have a homogeneous dispersion without evident aggregation and a relatively narrow size distribution less than 10 nm. The HRTEM shown in Fig. 3b delineate the average size of CQDs to be 4 ± 1 nm and the interplanar spacing between the carbon atoms was estimated to be 0.32 nm, which corresponds to (002) planes and resembles with XRD results (Jiang et al., 2009). SAED pattern indicates the amorphous nature of CQDs, which is also consistent with XRD.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4 a, untreated AgNPs exhibit an SPR absorption band at 427 nm, while Ag-CQDs NCs exhibit a distinctive localised surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption maxima at 423 nm (Fig. 4a).The presence of a blue shift within the absorption peak indicated that the CQDs had been successfully linked to the Ag NPs. Furthermore, the widening in absorption band of Ag-CQDs NCs can be explained to the Ag NPs growing in size due to contact with CQDs. The UV–Vis spectra of Ag-CQDs NCs at 247 nm and 423 nm, indicates that composite of CQDs and Ag NPs is successfully prepared.

The FT-IR spectrum of the Ag-CQDs NCs (Fig. 4B) showed absorption bands at 1142, 1460, 1541, 1692, 3617, and 3740 cm−1 commensurate with C–O, COO-, CC, CO, C–H, N–H and O–H vibrations, respectively, which verified the efficacious preparation of the CQDs-Ag NPs. As a result, metal ions can interact with Ag-CQDs NCs with numerous COO- and –OH groups..

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing potential of Ag-CQDs NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CQDs dispersion was used to investigate the selectivity of various metal ions. Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+ and Hg2+ were combined with 2 mL of Ag-CQDs NCs solutions by moderate shaking (every metal ion concentration was 20 μL). After a 2-min reaction, the UV–Vis spectra were measured. The addition of Ag-CQDs NCs composite solution within the metal ions does not show any instant result as shown in Fig. 5 a, however after 24 h it show reduction in absorption spectra of metal ions, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. Only Hg2+ ions demonstrate a diminution in the absorption edge of Ag-CQDs NCs, resulting in a visible change in color from brownish-yellow to colourless solution among the metal ions investigated. The absorption band of other metal ions, such as Co2+, is likewise slightly reduced. In comparison to other metal ions (Co2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Na+), Hg2+ induced a significant fluorescence quenching of CQDs. As the additional metal ions could not cause significant changes in the colour and UV–visible spectra of biogenic Ag-CQDs NCs, Hg2+ might be identified specifically using a UV–visible spectrophotometer or maybe even the bare eyes when Ag-CQDs NCs were used as a colorimetric sensor.

To confirm the specificity and selectivity of the synthesized Ag-CQDs NCs towards the detection of Hg2+, interference studies were carried out by adding 20 μL of potential competing ions such as Co2+, Cd2+, Na+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, and Mg+ to a solution of Ag-CQDs NCs + Hg2+ (Fig. 6 ). However, negligible changes were observed in the absorption intensity of the Ag-CQDs NCs + Hg2+which confirms the selectivity towards Hg2+.

CQDs' optical absorption properties are extremely helpful in the design of heavy metal optical devices that rely on colorimetric, absorbance, and photoluminescence concepts. The carbon framework of the CQDs employed in the nanocomposite's structure was functionalized with a huge proportion of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing (–OH, –NH2 and–COO–) groups that could bind effectively to Hg2+ ions. The ligand-metal complex formation (Ag-CQDs NCs/Hg2+) leads to the aggregation of Ag-CQDs NCs which is attributed towards the discoloration of brownish-yellow color of Ag-CQDs NCs and thus give a visible change for the recognition of Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of the different reaction parameters

ce:section.ce:para: To begin, the absorption characteristics of the obtained Ag-CQDs NCs were tested in aqueous phase of HCl or NaOH at various pH values (Fig. 7 ). To test the effect of pH on the systematic response of the anticipated Hg2+ sensor, an aqueous solution of HCl or NaOH was used to change the pH of a series of similar Ag-CQDs NCs colloidal containing 100 μL Hg2+ within 4–10 pH. The UV–visible spectroscopy of the mixtures were obtained after a few minutes. Due to the presence of functional groups such as carboxylic (-COOH) and amine (NH2) having electron-withdrawing and -donating properties on the surface of carbon dots make them very sensitive to pH change.

Hydrogenation of the amino groups causes the dissociation of CQDs from Ag NPs and the aggregation of bare biogenic Ag NPs. On the other contrary, protonation of carboxylate clusters in Ag-CQDs NCs surfaces minimizes repulsive forces between the NPs, increasing CQDs-Ag NP self-aggregation. According to the findings, a pH of 5 was determined to be appropriate for detecting Hg2+ utilizing Ag-CQDs NCs as a colorimetric sensor, as shown in Fig. 6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentrations of Hg2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Different ratio of conventional Hg2+ were introduced to a variety of Ag-CQDs NCs solution, and their UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. 8 .The preliminary injection of Hg2+ ions (20 mL) diminishes the absorption spectra of Ag-CQDs NCs marginally, and the absorption spectra of Ag-CQDs NCs is further reduced with subsequent additions. The degree of Ag-CQDs NCs aggregation was related to the concentration of Hg2+, according to the findings.

CQDs were synthesized successfully by green synthesis route via pyrolysis using syzygium cumini leaves. Spherical CQDs with size ranging from 5 to 15 nm are obtained. The formation of CQDs is confirmed by UV–Visible spectra, FT-IR, XRD and TEM. The UV peak of as-synthesized CQDs found at 217 nm which confirms the formation of carbon nano dots. FT-IR spectroscopy measurements confirm the presence/formation of functional groups on the surface of CD-NPs. Furthermore, utilizing metal interacting molecules present in the extracts, Ag-CQDs NCs have been effectively demonstrated as an excellent colorimetric sensor for the recognition and measurement of Hg2+ metal ions over a wide pH range. The work encapsulates the use of a waste as renewable feedstock (green chemistry principles 1 and 7), avoids the use of toxic reagents and auxiliaries (green chemistry principles 3 and 8). Thus, this study represents a step forward in the development of green/sustainable/cost-effective sensing platform for detecting heavy metal ions.

Rajdeep Kaur: Methodology and original draft preparation. Avtar Singh Matharu: Part-conceptualization, Writing, Reviewing and Editing. Jagpreet Singh: Conceptualization, Supervision, Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The RK and JS gratefully acknowledge Chandigarh University, Mohali and Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University, Fatehgarh Sahib, Punjab (India) for research lab facilities. JS would like to dedicate this work to the late Prof Mohit Rawat (Former Head Department of Nanotechnology, Sri Guru Granth Sahib World University), under whose supervision this work was carried out.
